court: Peshawar High Court ABBOTTABAD BENCH (Judicial Department)
bench: Additional District Judge-II, Mansehra in Civil Appeals No. 39/13 of 2010 and 40/13 of 2011
case: C.R No. 71-A of 2013 Syed Muzammal Shah & others Vs Gohar Aman & others
remarks:
West Pakistan Land Revenue Act, 1967 (XVII of 1967), Ss. 40, 53 & 172, West Pakistan General Clauses Act, 1956, S.2(41), Civil Court jurisdiction, special revision of record of rights, validity of notification, mala fide in law, colourable exercise of statutory authority. Civil Court jurisdiction despite statutory bar – Section 172 of the Act bars the jurisdiction of Civil Courts only in respect of matters which the revenue authorities are empowered by the Act to determine. The provision was never intended to place beyond judicial scrutiny every action purportedly taken under the Act. Even where a statutory bar exists, Civil Courts continue to exercise jurisdiction where the impugned action is alleged to be without jurisdiction, mala fide, coram non judice, or in excess of powers conferred by statute. Where mala fide, excess of powers, transgression of jurisdiction, colourable exercise of authority or ultra vires action is alleged, the Civil Court remains the Court of ultimate jurisdiction competent to examine the validity of the impugned action. Sections 53 and 172, read together, preserve the right of a person aggrieved by entries in the record of rights to institute a suit for declaration. (Paras 11, 12, 15 & 18)
Section 40 – Scope and limits of special revision – Section 40 confers an extraordinary supervisory jurisdiction intended to ensure accuracy and reliability of revenue records. It was never designed as a mechanism for reopening settled rights, nullifying judicial findings, or altering proprietary interests through executive fiat. A record of rights records existing rights; it does not create them. Revision of a record of rights is intended to ensure correctness of entries; it is not a substitute for adjudication of disputed title. The validity of an exercise under Section 40 depends upon the justification found in each case and remains subject to judicial scrutiny. (Paras 21, 22 & 23)
Notification under Section 40 – No recommendation by revenue authorities – No reasons recorded – Jurisdictional defect – The local revenue authorities did not recommend special revision. The Deputy Commissioner did not recommend special revision. The Board of Revenue itself initially doubted applicability of Section 40. Yet, notwithstanding these reservations, the impugned notification came to be issued. The notification itself furnished little assistance in explaining why the extraordinary jurisdiction under Section 40 was invoked. No reason whatsoever was disclosed. The notification neither identified any defect in the existing record nor referred to any fraud, mistake, irregularity, inquiry report, complaint, misrepresentation or other circumstance necessitating invocation of such extraordinary power. The notification recorded the conclusion but not the reasoning. (Paras 27, 28, 32 & 34)
Corrigendum – Expansion from village to estate – Effect – A notification under Section 40 contemplates special revision of the record of rights of a revenue estate and not of particular persons, selected khasra numbers or isolated parcels of land. The effect of the corrigendum was not merely textual but substantive, as it sought to bring the notification within the statutory framework of Section 40. Even after issuance of the corrigendum, the exercise did not culminate in a general revision of the record of rights of the entire estate. Instead, the consequential changes remained confined to specific entries affecting the rights of the petitioners. The corrigendum reinforced the impression that the process ultimately adopted was neither transparent nor consistent with the object, scope and requirements of Section 40 of the Act. (Paras 37 & 38)
Revenue record – Longstanding entries – Presumption of correctness – Longstanding entries incorporated in successive Jamabandis carry a statutory presumption of correctness. Such entries are not lightly disturbed. Revenue entries may not be conclusive proof of title, yet longstanding entries repeatedly carried forward in successive settlements and Jamabandis cannot be brushed aside without compelling justification. It is equally well settled that the statutory presumption attaches to entries in the columns of ownership and possession and not to entries in the columns of Lagan or Kaifiyat. (Paras 41, 42, 43, 45 & 46)
Administrative action vis-à-vis prior judicial determinations – Rights and entries that have survived judicial scrutiny over several tiers of adjudication acquire a degree of finality which cannot lightly be disregarded by executive action. While the executive may exercise powers conferred by statute, it cannot, under the guise of administrative action, nullify or circumvent findings rendered by competent Courts. What could not be achieved through litigation cannot subsequently be achieved through administrative action. (Paras 49, 50, 51 & 52)
Notification – Publication in Official Gazette – Effect of non-publication – Evolution of jurisprudence – Section 2(41) of the West Pakistan General Clauses Act, 1956 expressly links the very concept of a notification with publication in the Official Gazette. The legal position emerging from the authorities is that publication in the Official Gazette is not invariably mandatory for every governmental act. However, where the governing statute contemplates publication, or where the notification is intended to affect, alter, diminish or determine the rights and liabilities of citizens, publication ceases to be a matter of form and becomes a substantive requirement. In such cases, publication serves the fundamental purposes of legality, transparency, notice and due process. (Paras 59, 60, 64 & 68)
Notification affecting proprietary and possessory rights – Publication mandatory – The impugned notification was intended to alter entries affecting proprietary, possessory and tenancy rights recorded in the revenue record. The case satisfied both conditions recognised by the Supreme Court: first, the statutory definition expressly contemplated publication in the Official Gazette; secondly, the rights and liabilities of citizens were directly and substantially affected. In these circumstances, publication was not a procedural formality. It was an essential prerequisite to the legal efficacy and enforceability of the notification. A document which has not been published in the Official Gazette, despite being intended to alter vested proprietary and possessory rights, cannot readily be accorded the status of a legally operative notification capable of sustaining the drastic consequences that followed. (Paras 70, 71, 72 & 73)
Section 40 – Cumulative defects – Mala fide in law – Colourable exercise of statutory authority – The local revenue authorities did not recommend special revision; the Deputy Commissioner did not recommend special revision; the Board of Revenue itself initially doubted applicability of Section 40; no reasons were recorded in the notification; the scope of the exercise was subsequently enlarged through a corrigendum; longstanding entries reflected in successive Jamabandis were sought to be disturbed notwithstanding previous litigation concerning the same subject matter; the affected persons were not afforded any meaningful opportunity of hearing; and the notification was never published in the Official Gazette. Viewed collectively, these circumstances left little room for doubt that the impugned notification and the consequential exercise undertaken pursuant thereto travelled beyond the lawful scope of Section 40 and could not be sustained in the eye of law. The cumulative circumstances raised a compelling inference of mala fide in law. The power conferred by Section 40 was employed for a purpose not contemplated by the statute itself and constituted a colourable exercise of statutory authority. (Paras 74, 75 & 76)
